_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 1
questions.code:
a = Student("Ada", 12)
b = Student("Ben", 9)
b.credits += 3
print(a.credits, b.credits)
questions.explanation: The two calls create different objects. Changing b affects only b, so a stays at 12 while b becomes 12.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 2
questions.prompt: What must be the first parameter of this instance method?
choices: self
class
value
new
questions.explanation: Python passes the receiving object into the first parameter. The usual name is self, and the method uses it to reach that object's value.

questions.title: Trace Classes & Objects
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: `Student` is the class. Calling it creates one object, and `__init__` stores the values that belong to that object. The resulting output is 'Ada 18'.

questions.title: Classes & Objects good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Use a class when data and behavior belong together.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use a class when data and behavior belong together. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
def __init__(self, name):
    self.name = name

def __init__(self, name):
    name = name
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A value without `self.` is only a local name. Store it on the object when another method needs it later.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Each object has its own data'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Creating a second Student does not copy or replace the first one. Each call to the class creates a separate object with separate instance attributes.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Creating a second Student does not copy or replace the first one. Each call to the class creates a separate object with separate instance attributes.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Set starting attributes inside `__init__`.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Set starting attributes inside `__init__`. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Write `self.attribute` for data stored on an object.
questions.explanation: Write `self.attribute` for data stored on an object. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Remember that each class call creates a new object.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Remember that each class call creates a new object. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Keep a method focused on one clear action.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Keep a method focused on one clear action. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
